meta.summary: ByteDance Seed officially launched Seed 2.0 and also exposed a Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview build on Arena, marking the clearest product artifact in the current set. Anthropic changed Claude Code economics by requiring extra payment for third-party harness usage such as OpenClaw starting April 4, while AgentScope 1.0 and VibeVoice surfaced as notable new research artifacts but without the benchmark evidence needed to rank them as clear breakthroughs.

items.title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via Agentic Tree Search
items.summary_short: AI is increasingly playing a pivotal role in transforming how scientific discoveries are made. We introduce The AI Scientist-v2, an end-to-end agentic system capable of producing the first entirely AI generated peer-review-accepted workshop paper.

featured_topics.headline: ByteDance Ships Seed 2.0 and Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview
featured_topics.summary_short: ByteDance Seed officially launched Seed 2.0. The announcement marks a major new model release from the company
featured_topics.why_it_matters: A named frontier-model release from ByteDance is strategically relevant for anyone tracking Chinese foundation-model competition and potential shifts in model quality, pricing, and ecosystem pull.
key_takeaways: ByteDance Seed officially announced Seed 2.0 through its own channels, making this an on-record model launch rather than a leak or benchmark rumor.
The company also released Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview on Arena, indicating ByteDance is putting an early variant into public comparative evaluation workflows.
The same announcement stream included ByteDance Seed's 2027 foundation-model campus recruiting push, a signal that the company is pairing product launch activity with continued talent investment.

evidence.title: ByteDance Seed 2027 Foundation Model Campus Recruitment is Now Open (Internships Included)
Dola-Seed-2.0-Preview Model Release on Arena

featured_topics.headline: AgentScope 1.0 Launches as a Framework for Agentic Application Development
featured_topics.summary_short: Driven by rapid advancements of Large Language Models (LLMs), agents are empowered to combine intrinsic knowledge with dynamic tool use, greatly enhancing their capacity to address real-world tasks.
featured_topics.why_it_matters: A 1.0 release in agent infrastructure can influence enterprise build-vs-buy decisions, but only if it demonstrates production-grade orchestration, observability, and tool-use reliability.
key_takeaways: AgentScope 1.0 is positioned as a developer-centric framework for building agentic applications, targeting workflows where LLMs combine intrinsic reasoning with external tool use.
The release surfaced as a distinct versioned artifact—1.0—suggesting the project is moving from experimentation toward a more stable platform posture.
Based on the available evidence, the announcement emphasizes architecture and developer experience rather than publishing hard benchmark wins against competing agent frameworks.

evidence.title: AgentScope 1.0: A Developer-Centric Framework for Building Agentic Applications

featured_topics.headline: VibeVoice Technical Report Introduces Long-Form Multi-Speaker Speech Generation
featured_topics.summary_short: This report presents VibeVoice, a novel model designed to synthesize long-form speech with multiple speakers by employing next-token diffusion, which is a unified method for modeling continuous data by autoregressively generating latent vectors via diffusion.
featured_topics.why_it_matters: If validated, a model that improves long-form multi-speaker synthesis could matter for media generation, dubbing, and conversational audio products where coherence over extended outputs is a bottleneck.
key_takeaways: VibeVoice proposes long-form speech synthesis with multiple speakers using next-token diffusion, a design intended to unify autoregressive generation with diffusion-based latent modeling.
The report specifically targets continuous long-horizon audio generation, an area where speaker consistency and temporal stability are often weak points in existing systems.
The currently available description is technical and architectural; it does not yet provide enough benchmark detail in the evidence here to establish a clear state-of-the-art claim.

evidence.title: VibeVoice Technical Report

featured_topics.headline: Anthropic Changes Claude Code Pricing for OpenClaw and Third-Party Harnesses
featured_topics.summary_short: Anthropic changed its Claude Code subscription policy so that using OpenClaw and similar third-party tools will require additional payment. The change takes effect on April 4 and increases the cost of that workflow for subscribers
featured_topics.why_it_matters: This policy shift affects the cost structure of developer tooling built around Claude, which matters for budgeting, partner strategy, and the viability of third-party agent wrappers.
key_takeaways: Anthropic told users that beginning April 4 at 3 PM ET, Claude subscription limits can no longer be used for third-party harnesses including OpenClaw.
The change means Claude Code subscribers who rely on OpenClaw or similar tools will need to pay extra, directly raising usage costs for some developer workflows.
The move was independently reported by both The Verge and TechCrunch, suggesting this is a material platform-policy change rather than an isolated customer-support issue.

evidence.title: Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage

topics.headline: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
topics.summary_short: In January, folk artist Murphy Campbell discovered several songs on her Spotify profile that did not belong there. They were songs that she had recorded, but she'd never uploaded them to Spotify, and something was off about the vocals.
topics.why_it_matters: This other surfaced across 1 independent source(s).

evidence.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll

x_buzz.title: Show HN: sllm – Split a GPU node with other developers, unlimited tokens
x_buzz.summary: Running DeepSeek V3 (685B) requires 8×H100 GPUs which is about $14k/month. Most developers only need 15-25 tok/s. sllm lets you join a cohort of developers sharing a dedicated node. You reserve a spot with your card, and nobody is charged until the cohort fills. Prices start at $5/mo for smaller models. The LLMs are completely private (we don't log any traffic). The API is OpenAI-compatible (we run vLLM), so you j...
